data.title: Endeavour Silver: Approaching Its Free Cash Flow Inflection Point
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground.

Q1 Production

Endeavour Silver (NYSE:EXK) released its Q1-25 results last month, reporting quarterly production of ~1.21 million ounces of silver and ~8,300 ounces of gold or ~1.9 million SEOs. This translated to an 18% decline in payable gold and silver production year-over-year, impacted by lower silver grades and recoveries and a decline in throughput at Guanaceví (Mexico) in addition to lower gold grades at Bolañitos. The result was that Endeavour Silver was one of the few companies to see declining revenue year-over-year despite record gold prices and a much higher silver price ($31.99/oz vs. $23.47/oz), and this was despite higher ounces sold than produced in the period.

Starting with Guanaceví, production came in at ~1.02 million ounces of silver and ~4,000 ounces of gold at $26.50/oz AISC, down from ~1.19 million ounces of silver and ~4,100 ounces of gold in the year-ago period. As noted, lower production was impacted by a decline in throughput (~102,400 tonnes) and lower grades (348 G/T of silver vs. 402 G/T of silver) and the asset has seen a consistent decline in reserve tonnes over the past few years.

In fact, reserve tonnes have slid to just ~459,000 tonnes (year-end 2020: ~1.09 million tonnes), with the current reserve base supporting less than two years of mine life assuming a 1,000 TPD throughput rate. This is not ideal (assuming we don't see meaningful reserve additions) given that this is a ~5+ million ounce per annum asset on a silver-equivalent basis that's set to offset a sizable chunk of the growth coming from Terronera (~7.0 million SEOs per annum over LOM) when it heads offline.

As for cost performance in the period at Guanaceví, AISC jumped 21% year-over-year despite lower sustaining capital spend and will remain elevated given what's likely to be increased capex through the remainder of the year. Meanwhile, direct operating costs per tonne surged to ~$311 vs. ~$260 (Q1-24), impacted by the higher cost of purchasing third-party material for processing, higher royalties and the higher special mining duty, with the Mexican Government adding another 1% special mining duty rate starting January 1st, 2025.

On a positive note, the mine is at least generating positive cash flow given the higher metals prices even if costs continue to creep higher at this mature asset (producing since 1991, but mining in the district dates back to the Spanish in the 1500s).

Looking at Bolañitos, production came in at ~190,500 ounces of silver and ~4,300 ounces of gold, translating to a 53% increase in silver production, offset by a 28% decline in gold production and a significant dip in SEO production year-over-year. Lower production was related to mine sequencing with much lower gold grades (1.43 G/T of gold vs. 1.94 G/T of gold) offset by higher silver grades. Unfortunately, this resulted in a meaningful increase in AISC relative to H2-24 levels despite the record gold price given the impact of lower gold by-product credits, as well as higher royalties, the added special mining duty, offset by more silver ounces sold.

Financial Results

Digging into Endeavour Silver's financial results, they were underwhelming relative to peers, to say the least. Revenue came in at ~$63.5 million (Q1-24: ~$63.7 million) which was down marginally year-over-year and operating cash flow sunk to ~$3.4 million (Q1-24: $4.6 million) despite higher metals prices. The result was a free cash outflow of ~$45 million, mostly affected by the significant decline in silver ounces (~1.22 million vs. ~1.75 million) and gold ounces (~8,500 vs. ~10,900) sold in the period.

Given the continued cash outflows, Endeavour Silver's cash position sunk to ~$65 million, while working capital plunged to ~$15 million vs. ~$79 million heading into 2025. On a positive note, Terronera construction is near completion, and we should see a downtrend in capex going forward. The other good news is that higher silver prices have saved the day to improve the outlook for Q2-25 cash flows and get Terronera past the finish line.

That said, Terronera is going to need to operate as planned, especially given the significant burden of 68,000 ounces of gold hedged, with Endeavour absorbing ~$20 million in net debt from the Kolpa acquisition and having $120 million outstanding on its senior secured debt facility (2031 maturity).

As for its free cash flow profile, the company is finally nearing its inflection point, but the key to materially ramping free cash flow will be Terronera operating as expected. As we can see, Endeavour should generate upwards of $20 million in free cash flow this year after three years of significant free cash outflows, with the potential to generate over $120 million in free cash flow next year if Terronera operates at/above expectations. If all goes to plan, this will help Endeavour Silver join the ranks of other silver producers that have flipped to positive free cash flow, though with some (including Endeavour Silver) resorting to dilutive M&A to help accelerate this process.

Costs and Margins

Looking at costs and margins, Endeavour continues to be a high-cost producer relative to peers, reporting Q1-25 AISC of $24.48/oz, up from $21.44/oz in the year-ago period. This was despite a weaker Mexican Peso that was a tailwind in the period and lower sustaining capital, with costs expected to remain elevated until Terronera is online given that Kolpa is also a higher-cost asset, reporting AISC of $22.80/oz in 2024 and set to see higher sustaining capital spend this year relative to 2024 levels. Hence, while operating cash flow will improve in the upcoming quarter with a new operating asset and higher metals prices, I wouldn't expect to see materially better cost performance, with a negative impact from the reversal in the Mexican Peso that has strengthened vs. the US Dollar (UUP).

Fortunately, AISC margins improved even if AISC has continued to climb, coming in at $7.51/oz (23.5%) vs. $2.03/oz (8.6%) in the year-ago period. These margins are still miles below the industry average, but should improve in 2026 if Terronera operates as expected, with what should be sub $14.50/oz AISC in 2026 (SEO basis) at Terronera even baking in material cost inflation vs. prior estimates.

Recent Developments

Kolpa Acquisition

Endeavour's recently announced acquisition of the Kolpa Mine in Peru closed in early May, with the mine set to contribute more high-cost ounces to Endeavour's Q2-25 results. The good news is that this has allowed for diversification across jurisdictions and brought a longer life asset into the portfolio, especially if Endeavour has some luck adding reserves on this large land package.

On a negative note, the hits keep on coming from a share dilution standpoint, with a $50 million bought deal at US$3.88 to fund the purchase and the issuance of additional shares as part of the cash/share consideration. The result is that Endeavour is now up to ~290 million shares from just ~140 million shares to enter 2020 and while the dilution as part of the most recent deal is excusable on its own for what was a more than reasonable purchase price, it's tough to swallow on top of ~30 million shares issued at US$2.01 under its previous ATM and another ~16 million shares issued at US$4.60 less than a year ago.

Long-Term Growth

In terms of long-term growth, Endeavour Silver's outlook has improved materially, with a new mine set to be brought into commercial production by year-end (Terronera, Mexico) and another already in production joining the portfolio (Kolpa, Peru). The result is that Endeavour should see growth towards 18 million SEOs next year, giving it an industry-leading growth profile vs. 2024 output of ~7.6 million SEOs. However, it's important to note that these two longer-life assets are largely being used to replace and offset lost ounces from Bolañitos/Guanaceví, which are on borrowed time and running out of reserves quickly.

The Guanaceví and Bolañitos mines are the Company's only current sources of mineral production. Current life-of-mine plans provide for a defined production life for mining at the Company's mines. The Company's operating mines have expected lives of two to three years based on current proven and probable reserves, current production levels and management's estimated conversion of resources to reserves."

Consolidated cash costs (excluding Terronera and Kolpa) on a per ounce basis are expected to be higher in 2025 compared to 2024, primarily due to lower silver production from these mines as they enter their final years of production."

- Endeavour Silver Filings

Hence, while we should see a material bump in 2026/2027 production, I would expect much of this growth to be erased by 2028, given that production from Kolpa, even in an expanded scenario (2,500 TPD expansion), will be largely offset by Guanaceví heading offline. Plus, the precious metals contribution from Kolpa vs. Guanaceví is much lower, with Kolpa being a silver-lead-zinc-copper asset vs. Guanaceví as silver/gold. Hence, for those interested in precious metals exposure, per share production and especially per share precious metals production is not making much progress looking out past 2026.

Terronera

As for Terronera, Endeavour initially noted on the approval of construction that the mine would be in production by Q4-24, with commercial production by early to mid-2025 based on its 3-6 month ramp-up timeline. Unfortunately, the mine remains well behind schedule, with the updated outlook being a 90-day ramp-up period from May 1st (implying August 1st), with what will be lower grades fed to the plant to ensure the company is not wasting any high-grade ore as it ensures everything is operating smoothly.

Endeavour expects a 21-month construction period, including 3 to 6 months ramp up to full production with initial production expected in the fourth quarter of 2024."

- Endeavour Silver, April 18th, 2023

Unfortunately, this has meant no update yet to guidance for costs or production at Terronera and with it being over two years now since the last cost update, I wouldn't be surprised to see material cost inflation on both sustaining capital and operating costs at this asset. This isn't a deal-breaker, and Terronera will still offer much lower cost ounces than its current portfolio. Still, it's possible actual costs and updated estimates vs. what investors have been banking on from the 2023 study could be a negative surprise.

The good news is that wet commissioning began in early May (previously guided for "early Q2") and the first slurry was introduced to the concentrate filter. Endeavour noted that preliminary performance was consistent with design expectations. In terms of additional disclosure provided on the call, comments weren't negative, but not particularly upbeat either, and we'll have to wait for the Q2-25 call to see if things are progressing more.

I mean - but before I give it to Don, ultimately, I'd say wet commissioning, it's hard to say how well it's going to date because it's a start-and-stop process. Ultimately, some days, we feel like you're making steps forward. Other days, you take a break. And I think that's just part of the nature of commissioning."

- Endeavour Silver Q1-25 Conference Call

Per Share Growth

Moving to the only metric that matters for precious metals producers, per share growth, Endeavour Silver continues to flail in this department.

Today, the growth of Terronera is being cheered despite the significant cost of bringing this asset online in the form of seemingly never-ending share dilution (~110% share dilution in the past five years). And while this bloating of the share count might be excusable if this industry-leading growth profile was sustainable, the impact of bringing Terronera online behind schedule and over initial budget near the tail end of Guanaceví's life means that the higher production profile will not be sustained, resulting in a brief spike higher in per share metrics followed by a per share production cliff, as highlighted below (yellow line).

Worse, even during this spike, per share metrics are still markedly below 2018 levels, with zero evidence of per share growth here. And as stated in past updates, if a diversified producer cannot hold the line on per share metrics or grow per share metrics, an investor is better off simply holding gold/silver bullion, which will always hold its value vs. shares in producers that have a low regard for their currency and dilute at every turn.

The other significant cost of Terronera that seems to be ignored by the market for this gold-heavy asset is that Endeavour was required to hedge 68,000 ounces of gold or nearly two years of gold production from Terronera at $2,329/oz, meaning that even when this asset finally comes online, a meaningful portion of Endeavour's consolidated gold production is heading out the door at costs over $1,000/oz below spot levels. Hence, even if the steady stream of share dilution is finally in the rearview mirror, the negative effects of achieving this growth will still be felt for another two years, with gold forward settlements occurring from June 2025 to October 2027.

Valuation

Based on ~296 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$5.05, Endeavour Silver trades at a market cap of ~$1.49 billion, making it one of the lowest capitalization producers in the silver space today. This lower market cap relative to peers is largely attributed to its smaller scale (~16 million SEOs in 2026 on precious metals only), its less favorable jurisdiction profile vs. peers like Hecla Mining (HL) and its shorter weighted average reserve life. In fact, two of its current operations are staring down sub 2-year mine lives based on reserves, with just ~380,000 tonnes of material in the reserve category at Bolañitos, or barely 1 year of mine life.

Given the stronger gold/silver price environment and the lower hurdle for reserve conversion, there is certainly room to extend production into H2-26 and potentially further at these two assets. However, it's quite possible that Endeavour Silver could be down to just two assets (albeit stronger ones) by mid-2027, setting up a production cliff within just two years. Given this risk to the production profile and its reduced diversification vs. other investment vehicles that offer silver exposure like Triple Flag (TFPM) and Pan American Silver (PAAS), I think this discount continues to be justified.

So, What's a Fair Value for the Stock?

Using what I believe to be fair multiples of 11.0x cash flow and 1.4x P/NAV (8%) for a producer focused in Mexico/Peru (four operating assets in commercial production by 2026) with an inferior track record of per share growth offset by a silver premium, I see a fair value for the stock of US$4.90 (*). This continues to suggest that Endeavour Silver is fully valued at current levels and, given that I require a minimum 35% discount to fair value for Tier-2 focused producers, a significant pullback would be required to make the stock more interesting from a swing-trading standpoint.

(*) The Fair value estimate is based on a 65/35 weighting to P/NAV vs. P/CF. (*)

Obviously, a rising silver price could lift all boats and Endeavour Silver's leverage, industry-leading growth and improved diversification profile certainly make it a superior option to highly concentrated peers on sell signals like Avino Silver & Gold Mines (ASM). Still, I continue to see far more attractive bets elsewhere in the sector and think there are dozens of better options to a serial share diluter with inferior per share metrics like EXK.

Summary

Endeavour Silver saw a sharp decline in production in Q1-25 and while Q2-25 will be better with partial contribution from its new Kolpa Mine in Peru, investors will have to wait until at least Q4-25 to start to see meaningful contribution from Terronera. Looking ahead to 2026, free cash flow will surge, with Endeavour's EV/FCF multiple (2026) dropping closer to ~10x, a more reasonable multiple for a small-cap producer with the bulk of its NAV concentrated in Mexico.

However, this still doesn't offer enough margin of safety, and cheaper options are available elsewhere in the sector, especially when adjusting for overall quality and per share growth metrics. Hence, I don't see any way to justify chasing EXK above US$5.10, and I would view any rallies above US$5.35 before September as an opportunity to book partial profits into strength.

data.title: YMAX: You May Not Get Your Money Back
data.description:
The YieldMax Universe Fund of Option Income ETFs (NYSEARCA:YMAX) is an interesting product with attractive elements. I like how it has diversified its holdings so it is a "fund of funds" with less risk in any one stock or sector. The weekly income is also great, as is the 65% dividend. The downside is the consistent erosion in price, but income investors seem to tolerate that.

This article is more focused on one of the strategies I often hear about in the comment section or even in Seeking Alpha articles - with a dividend of 70%, if you hold for a year and a half, you'll have your money returned to you. After that point, you have completely de-risked and can leave your initial investment to pour in dividends. It sounds great, but the strategy has flaws.

YMAX Overview

YMAX is an actively managed ETF that holds around 32 other YieldMax funds at equal weighting.

These funds have a dual objective - to provide income and provide exposure to the underlying shares. They use an options overlay strategy to boost income, but this caps the gains in strong rallies.

YMAX itself doesn't use an options strategy, which makes the 1.28% expense fee high in my opinion. All it does is buy other ETFs and equally weight them. The income these funds provide is then distributed.

The Dividend

The distribution rate is currently listed as 70.28%.

This is based on a simple calculation:

The Distribution Rate is the annual rate an investor would receive if the most recently declared distribution, which includes option income, remained the same going forward. The Distribution Rate is calculated by multiplying an ETF's Distribution per Share by twelve (12), and dividing the resulting amount by the ETF's most recent NAV.

It's not the usual trailing twelve-month calculation, as that can be misleading in funds that have downtrending prices. Generally, as the ETF price drops, the distributions drop too. If you then include the larger distributions from 9 to 12 months ago, they distorted the calculation and make the yield seem higher than it really is. More on this later.

YMAX, therefore, uses the latest distribution and annualizes it, which can also be misleading, as the distributions are variable.

The distribution on 29th May was $0.2149, so the yield would have been around 80%. 4 weeks earlier, the distribution was only $0.1367, so the yield was 55%.

Getting Your Money Back

The variable distributions make it difficult to calculate when an investor will reach their goal and have all their initial investment returned. We could perhaps take an average of recent distributions and then annualize it, but this does not account for the fact that the erosion in NAV is likely to decrease the distributions over time.

A more reliable indication is adding up all the distributions since inception in February 2024. This is $11.4 in total, so with a buy-in point around $20, you will have had 57% of your investment returned in nearly 18 months.

Perhaps February 2024 was a bad time to buy, and YMAX distributions increased when they moved to weekly distributions. This is due to a larger return of capital, but in any case, we can do the same calculation for 2025 YTD. YMAX has distributed a total of $3.9 YTD. That will likely be just over $4.1 at the end of June. If you bought on the 2nd January at $17, you have had 24% of your initial investment returned in around 6 months. In around 2 years, you may have a chance of getting your initial $17 back in distributions.

This doesn't sound too bad, but there is one major caveat. This calculation relies on the distributions staying steady. However, in reality, the NAV is constantly eroding. How can YMAX continue to generate the same income from half the NAV?

NAV Matters

Quite simply, YMAX can't generate the same amount of income from a NAV of 13.5 as it did when the NAV was 20. It can, however, pay the same distribution if it chooses to. Indeed, it can pay double or even triple as it can return as much capital as it wants - there is no limit.

YMAX has managed to keep the distributions relatively steady even as its NAV has dropped. The recent payment of $0.18 is the same as the first payment of the year when the NAV was around 25% higher.

No matter how much income the fund has, it can top it up to around the same amount with ROC.

This is difficult to track, as the "tax documents" page on the YMAX site does not include a complete month-by-month breakdown of 19a-1 notices. In any case, from a seemingly incomplete list, we can see the June 2024 distribution had 29.95% ROC.

This jumped to 43.98% in October.

And the fund page now states:

The most recent distribution on 06/11/2025 contains 96.28% return of capital and 3.72% income.

So, it appears the ROC is increasing. It also appears the income is decreasing, and 3.72% of the $0.18 distribution means the income was only $0.067. Back in October, it was listed as $0.12.

I would like to track this in more depth, but an SEC search for all the filings from YMAX doesn't provide us more information. In any case, it is logical that more ROC will have to be used to keep distributions constant as the NAV falls.

This is not sustainable. If the funds that YMAX holds were to stay at a steady price, and YMAX continues to pay out around $0.18 a week with 97% ROC, there would be nothing left in less than two years. It's simply math.

Of course, the underlying funds may rally, but any pause or downturn would start to hurt the NAV again. Indeed, YMAX last made a recovery high on May 14th and is fading lower again as the rally in many of the ETFs it holds have slowed.

YMAX probably changes its strategy at some point to stop this NAV erosion. The obvious change would be to lower the distributions, but this would anger investors and could lead to outflows.

Conclusions

Receiving your initial investment returned in distributions is an attractive goal, but I think it's a rosy assumption and an unlikely outcome. Even if you bought YMAX at its launch, you would have 57% returned, and with NAV much lower, the fund's income is not what it was. It may take another year (or two) to reach the goal, by which time the distributions could be considerably lower.

YMAX has managed to keep its distributions consistently high, but has had to use more and more return of capital to do so. Its holdings need to rally strongly for this strategy to be sustainable. If they don't, all the fund's NAV will be returned to the next two years.

data.title: Pursuit Attractions and Hospitality: A Clean Balance Sheet, But A Fully Priced Ticket
data.description:
If you’ve never heard of Pursuit Attractions and Hospitality (PRSU), no worries—this isn’t some brand-new travel startup. We’re actually talking about the company formerly known as Viad Corp. (ring a bell now?). They’ve been a key player in experiential tourism for quite a while.

Actually, their story goes way back.

Right after the Great Depression hit, they started out running intercity buses under the name 'Motor Transit Corporation.' Not long after, they rebranded to 'The Greyhound Corporation,' bringing together around a hundred different bus lines under one roof.

It actually took them about thirty years to branch out into other areas like travel and recreation. By then, they were already using their bus network to cross-sell and drive more ticket sales—a pretty smart move for the time.

But the real turning point came in 1987, when they sold off Greyhound Corporation. That happened just a few years after they bought Glacier Park, Inc. (a former subsidiary of Great Northern Railway), marking a big shift in their focus toward tourism and experiences.

Upon acquisition, Viad began directly operating properties within Glacier National Park in Montana and Waterton Lakes National Park in Alberta, Canada.

The list includes a full package: historic properties like Glacier Park Lodge and Prince of Wales Hotel, as well as more modern ones like Grouse Mountain Lodge.

And that was just the beginning.

Over time, Viad expanded even further via M&A to the point that today they operate across three countries with about fifteen tourist attractions, nearly thirty hotels and lodges, plus convenience stores and restaurants—the kind of setup that, honestly, if my wife finds out about it, she’s booking the whole thing tomorrow.

More recently, the company’s gone through quite a transformation. They sold off their Global Experience Specialists division so they could double down on what they really do best: travel and recreation.

That’s actually when they decided to rebrand from Viad to Pursuit.

After the divestment, Pursuit became a pure-play company in the experiential tourism sector.

Along with that, they adopted the following approach:

  1. With the sale of GES to Truelink Capital late last year, Pursuit has taken a much more conservative approach to capital allocation. What we can see is that in FY 2024 alone they repaid $382 million of long-term debt, taking it to less than $20 million. That’s negative net debt when you consider that they are sitting on $22.8 million of cash;
  2. The logic here is pretty straightforward: when you’ve got a bunch of high-demand attractions in your portfolio, keeping them fresh isn’t just a nice-to-have—it’s a must. Renovations not only keep things looking sharp, they also help drive ROI by boosting guest traffic and, ultimately, revenue. Since FY 2015, Pursuit has grown revenue from its existing properties by 66%, and that momentum doesn’t look like it’s slowing down anytime soon. They’ve already mapped out $200 million in renovation projects to be completed over the next five years—with $38 to $43 million being spent just in FY 2025. That includes things like the 70+ brand-new suites in the Woodland Wing at the Forest Park Hotel in Jasper;
  3. Despite this more focused approach to renewals, that doesn’t mean M&A will go to waste. With the strong balance sheet after the sale of GES, Pursuit has freed up more room to add debt if it wants to—and especially if the right opportunity presents itself. For those who think they’ve stopped, just look at the past year. They’ve added Eddie’s Cafe and Mercantile, Avgar Lookout Retreat and Montana House (properties near their Avgar Village, Montana and within Glacier National Park), and Jasper Sky Tram (in the Canadian Rockies), the latter of which they plan to renovate soon. It seems to me that they’ve become more discerning about acquisitions, prioritizing, among many criteria, an IRR above 15%—which is good.

To keep it simple, here’s how we can group it all:

  • Canadian Rockies: They operate properties in towns such as Banff, Jasper, Waterton and Golden. Pursuit has high-volume attractions ranging from scenic chairlifts (Banff Gondola), boat cruises (Lake Minnewanka Boat Cruise and Maligne Lake Cruise), guided tours and even the highest suspension bridge in Canada (Golden SkyBridge). For lodging, they own the historic Prince of Wales Hotel (Waterton Lakes) and many others;
  • United States: Attractions in several states such as Alaska (Denali, Kenai Fjords and Talkeetna parks), Montana (Glacier National Park, acquired in the 1980s as I said before) and others. Accommodations near the parks and convenience stores and restaurants are also part of the portfolio here;
  • Iceland: They also have strong attractions like FlyOver Iceland (immersive aerial simulation) and the much talked about Sky Lagoon (geothermal spa near Reykjavík).

But even with a full portfolio, it seems that Pursuit didn't perform very well after taking off Viad's clothes and becoming a pure-play.

The stock is actually down 35.7% YTD, which feels a bit strange when you look at the numbers. Since Q1 FY 2024, Pursuit has beaten Wall Street’s revenue estimates in four of the last five quarters—and topped EPS estimates in three of them.

Yes, we all know the broader consumer discretionary space is going through a rough patch (just look at restaurants, retail, casinos), but you've got to wonder: did the market overreact with this sell-off?

To answer that, let's start by taking a look at the numbers.

A Look At The Numbers

Just like I mentioned in my Six Flags (FUN) write-up, Pursuit is a super seasonal business—Q2 and Q3 are where the magic really happens. That said, it doesn’t mean we should ignore Q1 FY 2025. It still gives us some insights, even if it’s not the star of the show.

They reported revenue of $37.6 million for the quarter—a modest increase of about 1% year-over-year.

However, Pursuit saw currency headwinds from the Canadian dollar that impacted approximately $1.3 million. Excluding those impacts, the company would report 4% year-over-year growth.

But what were the drivers of that growth?

The truth is, there was only one: Ticket revenue.

It was $19 million, which represented a 6% year-over-year increase.

The best part is that this growth was driven by both higher ticket prices (up approximately 10% year-over-year, with ETP increasing from $40.28 to $44.11) and visitor volume (459,500 versus 451,800).

The two biggest contributors to this growth in a low season were the brand-new Flyover Chicago (opening in March of last year, boosting attendance over the comparable period) and the expansion of the premium ritual experience at Sky Lagoon (boosting ETP from the premiumization).

Let’s talk about Rooms. Revenue from that segment was down 4% year over year to $7.3 million—but here’s the twist: all the key operating metrics actually improved. RevPAR jumped 9% to $67.56, up from $62.22. ADR rose 7%, and occupancy ticked up too, from 58.2% to 59.2%.

So, while topline sales dipped, the fundamentals underneath tell a much more encouraging story. The real drag here was the lowest room availability at the Forest Park Hotel I mentioned earlier. Once that's back to full capacity, we should see those numbers pick up again.

F&B revenue came in at $6.1 million, down 6% year over year. They didn’t spell it out on the call, but let’s be real, people are being more careful with their spending.

Retail revenue held steady at $1.7 million, flat compared to last year. Meanwhile, Transportation and Others brought in $3.4 million, slipping just 2.8% year over year. Nothing major, but it does show a bit of softness across the board.

After a record $55.6 million in SG&A (up 4.5% year-over-year due to seasonal expenses related to peak season preparation), Pursuit reported EBIT of -$31.3 million, down about $3 million from Q1 FY 2024. That gap widened to $6 million after the GES sale, leading the company to report a net loss of $31.1 million for the quarter.

That translated into OCF of -$24.4 million (up from -$7.5 million in Q1 FY 2024), and after Capex of $9.9 million, Pursuit reported FCF of -$34.3 million. They partially offset that by netting just under $5 million in debt, but by the end of the quarter they were left with a cash deficit of nearly $30 million.

But as I said earlier, Q1 isn’t exactly the best lens to see what’s coming next.

What really matters is the guidance—and yes, they did go ahead and reaffirm their outlook for FY 2025. So let’s dig into that a bit more.

They reported the following:

  • They are expecting double-digit annual revenue growth. Considering that they reported $366.5 million in revenue in FY 2024, we can estimate somewhere between $403.1 and $440 million (10% to 20% year-over-year revenue growth). Wall St. is targeting $408 million, which is also within the range I estimated;
  • A 27% to 40% year-over-year growth in adjusted EBITDA, targeting somewhere between $98 to $108 million. If in FY 2024 EBITDA was $61.7 million (about 80% of adjusted EBITDA), we can expect an EBITDA between $78.4 to $86.4 million for FY 2025;
  • Although not explicitly stated in the guidance, Wall St. sees a consensus EPS of $0.92 for FY 2025.

They’re counting on this growth coming from a few key things: Jasper National Park bouncing back, the new additions they made late last year (like Eddie’s Cafe and Mercantile, Avgar Lookout Retreat, Montana House, and Jasper Sky Tram), steady improvements in room performance, and ongoing renovations.

Pursuit Is A 'Buy' Or Are You Buying the Wrong Ticket?

Let’s see if Pursuit is actually a bargain after dropping over 35% so far this year.

I’m kicking things off with a Comps Model.

Projecting an EV of $956 million for FY 2025 and revenue ranges between $403.1 to $440 million, we would have an EV/S between 2.1x to 2.3x.

Comparing this to other comparable companies (please note that it is very difficult to find highly purchasable companies, so I will use other companies in the attractions and hospitality sector), we would have the following picture:

  • Vail Resorts (MTN): EV/S of 2.9x;
  • Carnival Corporation (CCL): EV/S of 2.2x;
  • Madison Square Garden Entertainment (MSGE): EV/S of 3x;
  • United Parks & Resorts (PRKS): EV/S of 2.6x;
  • Six Flags Entertainment: EV/S of 2.5x;
  • Dave & Buster's Entertainment (PLAY): EV/S of 2.1x.

The average EV/S of comparable companies is 2.5x. Adjusting for Pursuit (FY 2025 midpoint revenue of $421.5 million, net debt of -$5.7 million, and shares outstanding of 28.2 million), we get a price target of $37.50.

This would give us an upside of approximately 36% from here.

But of course, this isn’t the only angle we can look at. With the same projected EV and a midpoint EBITDA of $82.4 million, we would arrive at an EV/EBITDA multiple of 11.6x. This is higher than the industry median multiple of 9.9x.

Averaging across the control group, we would arrive at an average EV/EBITDA of 9.4x. Using this multiple in our model and plugging in the projections for Pursuit, we would arrive at a price target of $27.50.

That is roughly where the stock is trading today.

If you go by what Wall Street’s saying, it looks like they’re focused on EV/Sales, with a consensus price target hovering around $40.

I'm not quite on the same page. I think EV/EBITDA paints a more realistic picture here, and based on that, the stock looks more fairly valued right now. That said, if we split the difference between the two models, we land at a price target of about $32.50—still an upside of roughly 18%. However, that drops to $30 when you consider Wall St.'s revenue forecasts.

But here's the thing: none of that really excites me. And I'll tell you why.

Pursuit has major renovations lined up, which means heavy Capex ahead. That’s going to weigh on free cash flow. On top of that, it’s a seasonal business and leans heavily on the consumer discretionary cycle—both of which add risk.

So, even with a potential 18% upside, it’s not exactly compelling. The stock isn't dirt-cheap, and without dividends or buybacks to sweeten the wait, it just doesn't strike me as the kind of place I want to park my money right now.

That said, the stock’s not overpriced either. It’s trading more or less where it should be, which is why I’m landing on a 'Hold' rating for now.

If we pencil in a price target around $30, we’re looking at a margin of safety of less than 10%. Not quite enough to get excited about, but also not a reason to sell yet.

data.title: MP Materials: Vertical Integration Unlocks Value In A Volatile Rare Earth Market
data.description:
Introduction:

In today's article, I will be looking at MP Materials (NYSE:MP), a company that has been largely under covered for years up until recently when it burst onto the scene of many investors. I, however, have been following this company since its IPO and have grown a strong interest into this story, it's management team, and the global potential for the rare earth industry. Unlike most of the companies I follow, this is a small-cap company that has historically been extremely difficult to construct a proper valuation on. Since its initial public offering in 2017, this company has accomplished more than I think anyone expected, and has delivered exceptional operational advancements and I believe is nearing the inflection point from a small mining focused company to a three segment, fully integrated monopoly controlling the American rare earth industry that is capable of disrupting its global markets. In turn, setting a new standard as the low-cost producer of refined rare earth metals and the highest grade NDPR magnets. Today I will be covering where the company currently is operationally, where the global market is, it's superb and visionary management team, as well I will be placing an initial valuation on the company. My goal is to allow readers to better understand why this company has gained national attention as of recent and where it can go in 2025 and beyond. In this article, I rate MP Materials a strong hold after its recent YTD price appreciation, with an ideal buy zone in the $26–$28 range. I would not encourage buying this stock at these current prices, but for those interested in the company, I encourage you to stay on the sidelines and follow along as the story continues to unfold.

Background:

MP Materials was founded in 2017 after acquiring the Mountain Pass mine in California. This site is the only rare earth mining and processing facility of scale in the United States. Since then, MP has steadily evolved from a niche mining company into a vertically integrated player across the rare earth supply chain. While global attention often centers on China’s control of rare earths, MP has quietly built the foundation to emerge as a strategic counterweight.

The company began with upstream production, selling rare earth concentrate to partners abroad. Over time, the company has reinvested those profits to expand downstream production efforts. Today MP not only operates the Mountain Pass mine but is also ramping up refining and magnet production at its new facility in Fort Worth, Texas in 2025 and expects full operations in 2026. This transition marks a turning point in the company’s business model. MP aims to become the first domestic producer of both separated rare earth oxides and finished NdFeB magnets, which are used in electric vehicles, defense technologies, and advanced manufacturing.

MP is led by CEO James Litinsky, who has guided the company with a long-term vision after rebuilding the mine from former ownership's bankruptcy. He has created a company of operational excellence while navigating through extremely volatile mineral markets along with predatory pricing tactics by global competitors. In under a decade since the reopening of the mine, he will have built out a three segment business operating in multiple states. What has impressed me the most is balance sheet management by CFO Ryan Corbett. Financing the growth and expansion MP has experienced since 2017 while still maintaining only a ~100 million net debt position is no easy task. It's rare to find a high-growth company that pairs its expansion strategy with such a well-managed balance sheet. As an investor, that combination is both compelling and reassuring.

Global Market and Rare Earth Demand:

The rare earth market sits at the crossroad of the AI revolution, energy transition, national security, and dozens of downstream industries dependent on these critical metals. Among the 17 rare earth elements, neodymium (Nd) and praseodymium (Pr), combined as NdPr oxide, have become the most economically critical due to their use in ultra-high strength permanent magnets. These power electric vehicle drivetrains, wind turbines, robotics, medical devices, defense systems, etc. Without these critical metals, the advancement of EV markets, robotics, physical AI applications, and cutting-edge defense and aerospace technologies would not be possible.

In a recent mineral report from Adamas Intelligence, they expect NdFeB magnet demand is expected to grow at an 8.7% compounded annual growth rate over the next decade, brought forward by double-digit growth for robotics, air mobility and electric vehicle sectors, which will also translate to comparable demand growth for the rare earth elements (i.e., Neodymium, dysprosium and praseodymium) these magnets contain. Industrial applications, wind turbines, and consumer electronics are also driving usage higher. According to Argus Research Group, global demand for neodymium and praseodymium is set to outstrip supply through the early 2030s, with significant shortages expected unless new refining and magnet production capacity is developed outside China. This looming global supply and demand imbalance has been highlighted by MP Materials on multiple earnings calls.

Today, China controls over 85% of rare earth refining and more than 90% of global magnet production. China has historically been accused of weaponizing access to rare earths and therefore has the ability to create supply disruptions which would have ripple effects across energy, defense, and industrial sectors globally and in the United States. MP Materials is currently the only scaled end to end rare earth producer in North America and is one of just a handful of companies globally working to close the processing and magnet manufacturing gap.

What makes MP especially compelling is the scope of its vision. Rather than remaining a raw material exporter, the company is executing a three-stage model. Mining and beneficiation to then oxide separation on-site at Mountain Pass, California, and downstream magnet manufacturing at its new "Independence" Facility in Texas. Once ramped up to projected full run rate, MP is projected to produce 6,075 tonnes of NdPr oxide annually, which will be more than 9% of expected global supply. And while the Fort Worth plant will initially consume less than 10% of that total to produce 1,000 tonnes of high-grade NdFeB magnets, the company has built the independence facility to be able to scale this downstream capacity further over time. Which I fully expect for them to announce expansion projects with new foundational customers in the forthcoming years at the production facility.

With rare earth prices stabilizing after a post-2022 correction, I expect an upward trend from a bottom earlier this year. As well as foundational North American customers like General Motors already under contract, MP’s vertical integration positions it to capture value across the supply chain. Importantly, this isn’t just a commodity story. It’s a platform bet on American industrial resilience, and MP is building the backbone that no one else has been able to.

Where MP Stands Today:

2025 and 2026 are shaping up to be the most important years in MP Materials short history. After nearly a decade of planning, reinvestment, and infrastructure build out, the company has finally arrived at a turning point before the full realization of its three-phase strategy: mining, refining, and magnet production. This transformation comes at a pivotal geopolitical moment. In response to China's retaliatory tariffs, export controls, as well as price manipulation, MP made the bold move to stop all rare earth concentrate shipments to China, which represents a major share of revenue through sales to Shenghe Resources. Rather than sell into a market under 125% tariffs, the company is stockpiling material onsite and continuing to accelerate downstream operations.

The company’s California facility at Mountain Pass is now processing nearly half of its upstream production into separated rare earth oxides, particularly NdPr. This material is now being sold into more strategic markets including Japan, South Korea, and the U.S., which were previously inaccessible without downstream refining. MP has worked to broaden its customer base beyond raw material buyers to include industrial and manufacturing end-users who depend on refined inputs, which previously only have been supplied by China.

Later this year, MP expects to deliver its first batch of U.S. made NdFeB magnets to General Motors from its new Fort Worth “Independence” facility. This will mark the first time in decades that American mined and refined rare earths are used to produce high-performance magnets domestically at scale. This is an amazing accomplishment by the company and is one to watch out for later this year. Once the upstream mine & downstream facility are fully ramped, MP projects an annual run rate of 60,000 metric tons of rare earth oxide (REO) concentrate, 6,075 metric tons of NdPr oxide, and 1,000 metric tons of finished NdFeB magnets. While only ~10% of the company’s output will be used for magnet production, the strategic value of capturing every stage of the value chain cannot be overstated. This creates wonderful synergy and recycling opportunities not available to other producers globally. Giving them the ability to become one of the world's lowest cost producers. This is the moment MP Materials has been preparing for since day one, and is beginning to execute on everything it has once promised.

Valuation: Modeling MP Materials at Full Run Rate

To evaluate MP Materials’ mid to long-term potential, I modeled what the company could look like once all three phases of its vertically integrated strategy are fully scaled, which I believe to be possible in 2026 and is fully expected in 2027 under normal circumstances. That includes steady-state production from the Mountain Pass mine, full oxide separation in California, and commercial magnet output from its Fort Worth facility and full deliveries to General Motors. Using realistic production targets and expected mineral pricing, this approach reflects a grounded scenario that MP is actively building toward.

COO Michael Rosenthal has guided to annual production of 60,000 Metric tons mined at Mountain Pass. From this 60,000, 5,400 metric tons of NdPr oxide and 35,000 metric tons of rare earth oxide (REO) concentrate will be mined and refined, and 1,000 metric tons of NdFeB magnets will be manufactured at the independence facility. Using market pricing forecast of $65/kg for NdPr, $6,000/MT for REO, and $70/kg for high-grade magnets, this translates into total revenue of $630 million.

Product Volume Price Conversion Factor Revenue
NdPr (MT) 5,400 $65/Kg 1,000 $350 M
REO (MT) 35,000 $6,000/MT 1 $210 M
NdFeB Magnets (MT) 1,000 $70/KT 1,000 $70 M

Broken out, NdPr oxide would contribute $351 million, REO concentrate $210 million, and magnets $70 million. These assumptions align with MP’s announced capacity goals and publicly stated ratios of downstream supply mix derived upstream mining.

From the total revenue of $630 million, I will apply a 35% EBITDA margin of total revenue which, I believe, is completely achievable with higher and more stable commodity prices, continued synergies, material recycling opportunities, and advanced refining technologies, etc. This margin would yield $220.85 million in total EBITDA. After this, I then applied a 25x EV/EBITDA multiple, which reflects MP’s unique geopolitical positioning, monopoly U.S. supply control, and national security relevance which has achieved national recognition and support from the Trump Administration. This is also consistent with other rare earth companies' current or expected multiples, which range from a 15–35 EV/EBITDA. Using a 25x, this results in an enterprise value of $4.417 billion. After subtracting $100 million in net debt, the implied equity value is $4.317 billion. With 163.76 million shares outstanding, the fair value per share lands at $33.11 with 2026 EBITDA forecast.

To help frame the potential range of outcomes, I ran a sensitivity analysis with 20% downside and upside cases on commodity and magnet pricing.

Case Revenue EBITDA EV (25x) Equity Value Share Price
Low $504.8M $176.7M $4.417B $4.317B $26.36
Base $631.0M $220.9M $5.522B $5.422B $33.11
High $757.2M $264.0M $6.600B $6.500B $39.69

With the recent large run up in share price of this company over the past few months, I rate MP a hold rating based on my valuation analysis. If it dips down into the $26 I will add on more and continue my position into the company as I do believe this company will grow to many multiples higher as they grow end use magnet production in the coming years with new foundational customers passed 2026.

Risk to Thesis:

One of the most obvious risks to the MP Materials thesis is its natural exposure to commodity price volatility in an illiquid market, particularly neodymium-praseodymium (NdPr) oxide. NdPr is the corner piece of MP’s products, and in 2024 the market did experience a sharp drop in realized pricing from $70/kg in 2023 down to $51/kg. This decline meaningfully impacted revenue and margins, and future fluctuations could create similar threats. These pricing swings are influenced by a host of uncontrollable external variables including interest rates, exchange rates, tariffs, and supply dynamics within China, which, as stated before, controls over 85% of global refining and more than 90% of magnet manufacturing. While MP’s move into refining and magnets will mitigate some price sensitivity by capturing more margin per unit mined, commodity pricing risk remains a vulnerability.

Another material concern stems from geopolitical instability and the company’s reliance on a majority buyer, Shenghe Resources of China, for the majority of its concentrate sales. Following retaliatory tariffs and export controls in early 2025, MP suspended exports to Shenghe and instead announced to stockpile concentrate and prioritize downstream integration ramp up. While this was a strategically aligned move in my opinion, it temporarily cuts off a major revenue stream and accelerates MP’s dependence on ramping up oxide and magnet sales. I will be very eager and curious to see next quarter earnings and expected announcements to see how they have continued to navigate this problem and how it has impacted their bottom line. With any delays in bringing downstream production capabilities online or signing new customer agreements, this problem could prolong MP's revenue streams, especially if macro headwinds persist or if competition from China intensifies as negotiations continue.

Finally, MP’s operational execution remains a key risk. The company is in the midst of building the first fully scaled U.S. rare earth supply chain in decades, which is a task that is both capital intensive and extremely complex with no previous western hemisphere model to follow. Delays in commissioning the Independence magnet facility, difficulty in maintaining consistent quality, or challenges in securing skilled labor or equipment could all affect MP’s ability to fulfill its long-term supply agreement. The company has outlined ambitious goals of reaching 6,075 MT of NdPr oxide production and 1,000 MT of NdFeB magnet output, but any underperformance in achieving these run rates would undercut the high valuation multiple investors may assign to full vertical integration. Additionally, MP's ability to convert new customer discussions into firm long-term offtake contracts remains unproven at scale other than the one foundational customer. In short, while the strategic upside is substantial, the execution path remains complex and subject to multiple industry and geopolitical headwinds which, in part, may be out of their control.

Final Thoughts:

As of mid-June 2025, MP Materials has had a successful year in the stock market. Currently, the company stands up over 100% year to date.

Operationally, in the first quarter, the company showed real progress. It’s now producing separated rare earth oxides at Mountain Pass in meaningful volumes, and it remains on track to begin delivering its first batch of domestically produced magnets to General Motors later this year. That’s a milestone worth paying attention to.

Looking at the broader picture, I continue to believe that MP is one of the most strategically important companies in America today. Rare earths are no longer just a commodity story. They’re tied directly to national defense, clean energy, advanced manufacturing, and future technologies. With that in mind, it’s hard to ignore the potential for government support in the years ahead, especially as this has been highlighted as a choke point in the United States and Chinese active trade negotiations. With President Trump announcing potential for government support in continuing to build out the rare earth magnet industry domestically. MP is in the best position to benefit if that happens. They’re building the exact type of domestic supply chain the government wants to see more of.

Now, let’s talk about the stock. It’s had a big run recently. After a strong year-to-date move, I think a lot of near-term optimism is already priced in, which can be noted from my valuation section above. That’s why I’m rating MP as a strong hold. I still believe in the long-term upside. I still think this company can become the backbone of a rare earth supply chain in North America. Personally, I would feel more comfortable adding to my position if it pulls back into the $26 to $28 range. For investors who don’t own it, yet, I’d suggest keeping it on your radar and watching closely over the next few quarters. This story is just getting started, and what happens in the back half of 2025 could be incredibly important.

data.title: JEPI: Flat Markets Would Make Them Outperform, But I Don't See That Happening
data.description:
With $40.02 billion in assets under management, JPMorgan Equity Premium Income (NYSEARCA:JEPI) is the largest ETF offered by JPMorgan (JPM). This ETF is an alternative to the JPMorgan Equity Income Mutual Fund (OIEIX), differing in that it utilizes derivative products to enhance the fund's yield. Currently, the ETF has a trailing dividend yield of 8.36%, and in my opinion, it is not worth initiating a position in it as flat markets are what really benefit their structured product, and therefore, in this analysis, I will rate the ETF as a "sell."

JEPI: A Massive Success for JPMorgan Asset Management

First, I would like to point out that JEPI is a relatively new product launched in 2020, and so far, it has been a success for JPMorgan Asset Management. As shown in the image above, monthly net flows have remained consistently positive since inception. Additionally, as illustrated in the image below, the AUM has continued to rise in an upward trend, despite paying high dividend yields that decrease the assets under management.

This rise in popularity benefits investors with lower implicit fees from bid-ask spreads, as higher volume in a particular security narrows the spread. Currently, over the past three months, the ETF has had an average daily volume of $290.23 million, and generally exhibits a pretty narrow bid-ask spread of 0.02%. Based on an annual expense fee of 0.35%, if investors buy the ETF and hold it for one year, they will be paying a one-year fee of 0.37%, which is attractive considering that the ETF is actively managed and has a team of portfolio managers and analysts around.

Investment Mandate

The ETF has a mandate that requires it to invest at least 80% of its assets in equities, with a tilt towards value large-cap companies in the S&P 500 that pay high dividends and are more defensive during economic downturns. The following 20% is utilized to sell call options through S&P 500 equity-linked notes, thereby generating an income.

The drawback of that 20%, utilized to follow a covered call strategy, is that by selling calls, the position will incur losses if the S&P 500 price rises. Of course, these losses are partly covered by an equity portfolio that has a beta close to one. However, if the S&P 500 drops, it would be beneficial to the derivative position, as income would be gained.

Although the ETF still has a massive exposure to beta, its structured composition allows it to be less volatile than the S&P 500, and therefore, it becomes a positive characteristic for income investors in retirement or for investors who seek a correlation of less than one to the equity markets.

Yield Difference in a Structured ETF vs Classical Income ETF

Fund JPMorgan Equity Premium Income JEPI JPMorgan Equity Income (OIEIX) Vanguard High Dividend Yield (VYM) Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity (SCHD) iShares Select Dividend (DVY)
4-Year Average Yield

8.69%

3.57%

2.95%

3.38%

3.48%

Source: Seeking Alpha

Although there are many popular dividend ETFs, such as the Vanguard High Dividend Yield ETF, the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF, and the iShares Select Dividend ETF, the difference in yield between a classical one and one that sells equity-linked notes ((ELNs)) could be considerable. For example, at an average yield of 8.69%, JEPI's yield exceeds the rest, with much lower averages below 3.6%.

Simultaneously, during the past five years, the price return of the JEPI has been pretty mediocre at 10.11%, which implies a poor annual rate of 1.94%. Nonetheless, the total return was much higher at 70.29%, but as expected, it underperformed the S&P 500 by 36.53%.

Potential JEPI's Performance During a Bull, Flat, or Bear Market

In my opinion, this is not an ETF for long-term holding, unless the investor is seeking reduced volatility and wants to enhance their yield while maintaining exposure to beta. Also, if an investor is willing to do this, this is a product that is highly tax inefficient. First, it is actively managed, so it increases asset turnover, and second, gains in derivatives would likely be considered short-term capital gains.

Meanwhile, I believe that this is an ETF more suitable for tactical opportunities. For example, when markets are expected to be flat, this ETF is likely to outperform the S&P 500, as gains will occur on the side of the derivative, and higher dividends from a tilt in value companies will outperform growth stocks.

Simultaneously, during a classical economic recession (not the one seen in Covid, where Tech outperformed), I believe the ETF would slightly outperform the S&P 500, again, due to the higher concentration of defensive companies, and due to the effects of the embedded derivative. Nonetheless, this gain from the covered call positions is limited, and therefore, I believe the outperformance would be only slight. Also, if there is a V-shape recovery, the gains from the derivatives won't be that substantial, and in return, there won't be a huge difference.

Last, not everything could be great, and if there is a trendy market to the upside, as it's the most normal condition in US equity markets, the ETF will underperform, providing investors with much less returns than holding a pure income ETF or the S&P 500.

JEPI: Perhaps Not A Great Alternative During The Current Landscape

As commented, what could potentially benefit JEPI and make it outperform the S&P 500 are mainly flat markets, with a slight gain during bear markets.

Although equity markets haven't exhibited large price changes over the past five weeks, there are several scenarios that could trigger a directional shift in the market again. Mid-term, it is hard to believe that markets will continue to be flat, especially with all the tariff concerns, negotiations that need to occur, geopolitical tensions, rising oil prices, and more scenarios that could cause markets to trend if situations improve or deteriorate.

Although this ETF could slightly outperform during bear markets, I believe the current landscape makes it different. This is because a potential economic impact from the effect of tariffs would most likely affect value stocks that are required to import from abroad. Potentially generating a divergence between value and growth stocks, creating another anomaly, as seen during the pandemic.

Therefore, considering all these factors, I do not consider the current landscape a great tactical opportunity for this ETF; thus, I will assign it a "sell rating."

data.title: Albemarle: This Minerals Stock Could Make A Good Portfolio Addition
data.description:
Introduction

Albemarle Corp. (ALB) is a global leader in lithium, bromine and catalysts with vertically integrated lithium operations. In a multi-year selloff that started in 2022, the stock is down more than 80% from its ATH, driven by depressed lithium prices and sector-wide pessimism.

Low lithium prices have led to declining revenues and negative earnings. Despite current sentiment, I believe that this drawdown is overdone and at current prices and crushed valuations, Albemarle offers a good risk-to-reward ratio for investors to go long.

Lithium Market - The Single Most Important Variable For ALB

In Q1 2025, sales dropped by 21% YoY, marking the continuation of a steep decline. Net income, however, was $41M, up from $2M in Q1 2024. Adjusted EBITDA "only" shrunk 8% YoY.

Prices of lithium carbonate dropped from ~$20/kg (in 2023) to ~$9/kg (at the end of 2024), which lowers both sales and margins. Lithium price is a complex result of the interactions of supply and demand. Demand-wise, lithium is used for lithium-ion batteries, needed for EVs, consumer electronics and energy storage systems. It also has industrial and pharmaceutical use cases. Batteries by far drive the largest share of overall demand, with EV batteries covering around 60-65% of all lithium demand. Therefore, demand of electric vehicles matters most for Albemarle. Contrary to what one might believe when seeing ALB's stock price, sales of EVs are up 34% YoY globally and 41% in China, the most important market for EVs.

This trend is expected to continue throughout 2030 and beyond, as EV penetration is anticipated to increase tremendously as regulations and incentives around the world emerge. Therefore, lithium demand is expected to rise sharply. Specifically, it is estimated to more than double until 2030. At the same time, supply is expected to remain in balance due to project delays and many companies not being able to operate or expand profitably at current prices. Even if supply grows as well, I believe it to be unlikely to grow as fast as demand, especially at low prices. Therefore, the gap between supply and demand will only widen if prices stay low. The wider the gap, however, the more likely it is for lithium prices to appreciate.

On the positive side, Albemarle also operates outside of lithium, in Specialties (bromine) and Ketjen (catalysts). These segments provide diversification and more stability. Together, they could make up approximately 40% of revenues in 2025. In contrast to Energy Storage (lithium), EBITDA of both other segments is up YoY, 30% for Specialties and 76% for Ketjen. At the midpoint, they also guide for low single digit EBITDA growth in these two segments for FY2025.

Further, Albemarle knows the impact low lithium prices have on their business. As a response, they are more disciplined with their expenses and investments. They hit 90% of their $350M savings target by April, slashed CapEx by 50% YoY ($750M guide at midpoint) and streamlined operations. They also guide for cashflow to be neutral in FY2025, which I like.

Valuation

When checking fundamental valuation, it is undeniable that ALB is historically low valued. Trading at 1.4X sales and 0.9X book value, they are below all-time historical averages on both metrics. This suggests that the market does not price in a recovery of lithium prices and expects them to stay low, creating opportunity for investors who believe otherwise.

Competitors Pilbara Minerals Ltd. (OTCPK:PILBF) and Sociedad Quimica y Minera de Chile S.A. (SQM) trade at forward P/S of 5.4X and 2.1X, respectively. Therefore, Albemarle also looks cheap on a peer comparison valuation, changing hands for just 1.4X forward sales.

In case lithium price increases though, analysts' revenue expectations should easily be beaten by Albemarle. Assuming prices of $13.5/kg in 2025 (the midpoint of their scenario guidance), Albemarle forecasts $5.7B of sales and $1.5B of Adj. EBITDA. This would put them at a Fwd P/S of 1.2X and EV/Adj. EBITDA of 6.1X just for their lithium business. In my view, these levels are unsustainably cheap and call for multiple expansion.

Further, Albemarle trades at a monthly RSI of 34. As depicted below, RSI has historically ranged from levels slightly above 30 to around 80 and moves in cyclical waves. 2022's ATH simultaneously had a monthly RSI of over 60, a few months after it was at 80. Current levels could mark the start of a new cycle slowly growing into monthly oversold levels. ALB is currently fighting for the ascending trend line starting in 2015. If this level gets lost, I expect to see further downside. They also touched the lower end of the monthly Bollinger Bands earlier this year which can offer strong support. For a real uptrend to manifest, they need to sustainably trade above the descending trend line starting in early 2024. Inflection of both trend lines suggests this might start to happen at the end of 2025.

Risks

Despite also selling other minerals than lithium, lithium prices are still crucial for ALB's stock development, whether it be actual increases in sales and earnings or just the anticipation of such, ramping up valuations. If prices stay depressed, however, and the expected price increase never occurs, then Albemarle stock will most likely head lower. Revenue declines will continue and profits and FCF will persist in the negatives. This would put them under financial distress, potentially causing them to dilute shareholders heavily and/or take on lots of debt, strongly increasing the risk of going bankrupt.

Conclusion

Albemarle today is a solid addition to an already well-diversified portfolio. They are very reliant on lithium price development which can be both opportunity and risk. Personally, however, I believe that ALB today is a solid risk/reward play due to its incredibly low valuation and the likely scenario that lithium prices will eventually rise in the mid-term. Until then, Albemarle has proven to keep expenses down, reducing CapEx in order to survive an unprofitable economic environment. Using acceptable amounts of dilution in addition to the aforementioned cost discipline, they should be able to make it until 2030 even amid depressed lithium prices. Until then, demand and supply should have created a large gap, increasing the likelihood of price adjusting to the upside.

data.title: Royce Small-Cap Trust: Buy This 8% Dividend Yield With Thematic Upside
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Popular investment themes prevalent in today's market, like the Mag-7 and tech-focused growth stocks, has left the Royce Small-Cap Trust (NYSE:RVT) largely ignored. Trading at a discount to its net asset value, the Trust currently boasts a dividend yield of just under 8% while providing investors with an opportunity to gain exposure to small-cap, value-oriented U.S. equities.

The Trust is built on fundamental principles that promotes the purchase of equities at fair prices to protect investors during various market cycles - all while consistently paying a dividend yield over 7%.

I have initiated a Buy rating and recommend investors include this name in their portfolio for its exposure to small-cap equities, consistent high-paying dividends, and a disciplined management team.

Fund Objectives

Created by the "legend of small-cap investing" Chuck Royce, RVT is a closed-end fund offering investors a chance to buy into the legend's investment strategy. Unlike private direct investments, a closed-end fund offers investors higher liquidity and transparency through the public markets.

The Small-Cap Trust was first established in 1986 and has been in operation for more than 38 years. Over this time period, RVT has grown at a compound annual growth rate of 9.65% when including the returns from dividends.

The purpose of the closed-end fund is to provide small-cap, high yield dividend exposure to investors in all economic cycles. RVT invests primarily in stocks with strong fundamentals at attractive valuations - the disciplined approach that Mr. Royce has embodied since the inception of the fund.

The fund's average P/E ratio from its underlying stock holdings is only 16.5x, which should give investors confidence that during periods of market turmoil, their high dividend yield and principal will be protected.

Historically, RVT has consistently outperformed the Russell 2000 during periods of market volatility.

The fund outperformed the Russell 2000 during nine out of 11 downturns of 15% or more. The median price drop for RVT during the market periods highlighted above was -26.4% compared with -32.5% for the Russell 2000.

The last few months' turbulent market performance makes the Small-Cap Trust a very compelling opportunity for investors looking to protect their principle while still collecting a large dividend.

The Trust's dividend payments are made on a quarterly basis - and investors have the option to opt in for dividends (receiving cash) or a distribution of additional shares (growing their existing share count).

RVT pays dividends at an annual rate of 7% of the average of the prior four quarter-end net asset values. If RVT is trading below its NAV when investors make their purchase (like it currently is), the yield will be higher than the target 7% distribution rate.

The next quarterly dividend payment on June 25th will be $0.29, implying an annualized yield of 7.88% - made up of short-term gains, long-term gains, and distribution of capital (under certain scenarios), all of which have different tax implications (but more on this later).

Performance, Fees, and Tax Considerations

Already briefly touched on, the Trust's NAV and market value have both outperformed the S&P Smallcap 600 and the Russell 2000 since the fund's inception, providing investors with the much-needed confidence that their share distribution or cash dividend is not only sustainable but outperforms its benchmark.

However, the Trust has recently struggled to keep pace with the S&P 500, but shareholders with a long-term orientation have done very well. The 30-year return has compounded at an annual rate of 10.27% when factoring in the dividend - slightly above the 9.65% compounded gain since inception, mentioned earlier.

RVT has maintained high returns on its fund through portfolio concentration in only a few sectors.

The Trust's top three sectors account for 61.4% of the total portfolio. RVT is currently overweight in these three sectors while being underweight in health care, real estate, and utilities.

Its overweight sectors, like industrials and information technology, are higher-growth areas, which give investors better opportunities to capture capital appreciation in addition to the regular dividend distribution. The Trust's total assets are made up of between 400 and 500 stocks at any given period, with an average annual turnover ratio of 40%.

An important aspect to any fund is the fee structure and transparency. While many funds provide vague summaries of their fee structure or include hidden fees, RVT's management lays out exactly how it makes money. The adviser's base fee is 1.00% and is subject to increases or decreases of up to 0.50% based on performance.

Additionally, the firm has a negative performance penalty wherein no fee will be taken for any month in which the fund's performance over the trailing 36-month period is negative. The advisers have created a structure that incentivizes management to perform for the benefit of its shareholders, which has driven its decades-long outperformance.

Naturally, investors may be unaware of the tax implications associated with a closed-end fund. RVT's fund structure is set up such that investors will receive a 1099-DIV form at the end of each year, requiring payment of the total proportionate tax from the fund's activity for the year.

Like an individual investor who buys and sells stock, RVT accumulates ordinary income and long-term capital gains. At 2024 year-end, the fund's ordinary income, taxed at higher rates, was 20.5% of total distributions while the long-term capital gain was 79.5% of total distributions. In this scenario, a Trust investor's 1099-DIV would include 20.5% of the tax on their ordinary income, while the 79.5% would be taxed at the much more advantageous 15% rate.

Capital Appreciation Opportunities

At the heart of my title is what I like to refer to as "thematic" drivers of capital appreciation. Historically, small cap and value stocks have outperformed over extended periods of time - a huge benefit for RVT. However, recent investment themes, including the Mag-7 and "growth at any cost" theme in the tech sector, have taken over the market.

With the recent performance of the Mag-7, investors are migrating out of small-cap stocks to more 'glamorous' large and mega caps on the promise of exponential returns, reminiscent of the "Nifty Fifties".

The chart from the CFA Society Blog highlights the recent underperformance in small caps relative to large caps - a 12-year trend that generally reverts back in favor of small caps during periods of high inflation or uncertainty.

The "growth at any cost" theme has reoriented investor's interest away from traditional value companies and back to high-growth tech companies. One of the Trust's core investment principles is finding attractive businesses at reasonable prices, which as an investment theme has become out of favor in the long and overextended bull market.

Ultimately, the prevailing themes in the market over the last few years have favored growth over value and large cap over small cap. But the data suggests that over the very long term, disciplined investors who are willing to stay the course and ignore the current market fad will experience the greatest returns.

Risks

The first risk I have identified is the 40% turnover per year. The advisers of RVT may have many reasons for reshuffling its securities portfolio, but the turnover poses potential burdensome tax consequences, as touched on previously.

Selling securities in the portfolio that have recognized significant gains, especially in a short period of time, may increase the risk of investors paying a higher percentage of the disadvantageous ordinary tax rate. Although management has done a nice job of keeping its ordinary income tax at 20.5% during 2024, there is no guarantee that this will remain low.

I classify this as a minor risk, expecting that it will have overall little impact on an investor's taxable distributions but recommend investors consider this for their individual tax purposes.

The second risk is opportunity cost, which I pose in the following question: "Why not just buy the S&P 500? It has outperformed the RVT fund since inception and has lower overall fees". This is a fair question - since 1986, the S&P 500 has returned ~6,500% while RVT has returned ~3,400% during the same period.

However, I would argue that each investor has different needs and lifestyle choices. While some investors may choose to place their portfolio in the S&P 500 and "sit and forget", other investors may choose to place their funds in RVT to capture dividend income to fund their immediate lifestyle.

The first risk considers the impact of taxable distributions at various tax rates based on fund turnover, while the second risk considers opportunity costs. I expect that both risks should pose only minor concerns for investors - something worth considering as part of an individual's unique investment portfolio and lifestyle choices, but a risk not impactful enough to deter prospective buyers from the fund.

Conclusion

The Royce Small-Cap Trust has a history of strong operating performance. Led by Chuck Royce for the last 38 years, the investment provides exposure to small-cap equities with high dividend payments.

The investment themes of the current market have made Royce's investment strategy look antiquated and are currently out of favor. However, the research shows that RVT's investment approach has historically outperformed the market.

I have initiated a Buy rating and recommend disciplined, dividend-seeking investors consider starting a position in the stock.

data.title: Devon Energy Remains Heavily Undervalued
data.description:
Devon Energy (NYSE:DVN) is a mid-cap hydrocarbon exploration company, with a market cap of just over $20 billion. We've discussed the company as undervalued before; however, the company has continued to be punished by weak oil prices. Despite that, as we'll see throughout this article, the company's growth makes it a valuable investment.

Devon Energy Portfolio

Devon Energy has an impressive and diversified portfolio centered primarily around the Delaware Basin.

The company's portfolio is majority oil at just under 50%, but the company also has a significant amount of gas and NGLs. The company's multi-basin portfolio enables it to be connected to multiple major demand centers and other export infrastructure. The company has a deep inventory to support this production, which we expect will lead to continued strength in cash flow.

In the company's key Delaware Basin, the company has walked down its rig count from 15 to ~12. However, the company managed to increase the wells online by almost 10%, and it's maintaining respectable upstream capital investment. Still, as a result of fewer wells, the company is seeing some decline in production volumes.

As an oil exploration and production company, the company has to play to the whims of the oil market and prices that it can't control, but an intelligent and well-tailored strategy is key.

Devon Energy Shareholder Returns

The company has a strong financial position with $1.2 billion in cash and net debt to EBITDAX of 1.0x (~$8 billion in net debt).

The company maintains strong investment grade credit ratings as a result of this financial picture. It plans to retire $2.5 billion in absolute debt, something it can comfortably afford, and it's continuing to maintain an attractive hedge position. The company maintains a strong liquidity position for any sort of downturn, which is impressive.

The company has updated its outlook with a 1% improvement in volumes to hit 385 thousand barrels / day, with capex declining by $100 million at the midpoint to $3.8 billion. The company's breakeven level is ~$45 / barrel WTI, well below current prices. Current WTI prices sit at $75 / barrel, boosted by Iran-Israel instability, a level where the company generates ~$3.6 billion FCF.

Versus an almost $22 billion market cap, that's a ~15% FCF, but the company's FCF yield drops towards ~$11% if you look at WTI prices just under $60 / barrel several weeks ago.

The company is targeting a 70% cash return in 2025 despite continued improvements to its balance sheet. It's worth noting, especially in a higher interest rate environment, that cash towards the balance sheet isn't worthless. It saves on interest expenses and enables the company to clean up its balance sheet in a tough market.
