The company has increased its dividend by a very respectable 9% to $0.24 / share. That's an annualized dividend of more than 3.3%, a strong place to start from. On top of that, the company plans a consistent share buyback of roughly $1 billion annualized, which by itself is an almost 5% yield.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is visible in the company's FCF profile. At $50 WTI to $70 WTI, the company's FCF goes up by $700 million per $10 / barrel WTI. A major downturn in oil prices, for example due to a nuclear deal with Iran that ramps up production, could push down prices and hurt future shareholder returns.

Conclusion

Devon Energy has seen its share price trend consistently downwards since peaking in late-2022. During that time, the company's share price has dropped by more than 50%. With weak oil prices due to trade wars and tariffs at the start of the year, oil prices were quite weak before the current Iran-Israel conflict.

That conflict remains incredibly uncertain, especially with regard to whether production actually gets taken offline. However, in a variety of pricing environments, Devon Energy's production remains incredibly strong. The company is focused on strong shareholder returns through dividends and share repurchases. Overall, as a result, we view the company as a long-term investment.

data.title: Dolly Varden Silver's Kitsault Valley Potential Seems Priced In For Now
data.description:
Dolly Varden Silver Corp. (NYSE:DVS) is a Canadian exploration company that targets high-grade Silver and Gold in the Golden Triangle in British Columbia. In this region, DVS has a consolidated 163 km² land package known as the Kitsault Valley Project, where DVS owns multiple historic mines. This includes their new high-grade Silver veins and Gold-rich zones, lying on Homestake Ridge. On paper, DVS seems like a pure-play Silver explorer at a time when industrial demand is rising, supply is tightening, and Silver prices are outperforming other assets. DVS is also drilling aggressively to expand its resource base of over 60 million ounces of Silver, plus around one million ounces of Gold. And going forward, DVS intends to achieve more discoveries, resource growth, and deposit connections that could unlock significant value. However, on balance, I don’t see a major opportunity on DVS at these levels, which is why I deem it a “Hold” for now.

A Silver Pure-Play

Dolly Varden Silver is a mineral exploration company that is focused on high-grade Silver and Gold deposits in British Columbia’s Golden Triangle region. This region also happens to be one of the richest producers of these minerals in the world. DVS was incorporated back in 2011 in Canada, and it’s currently headquartered in Vancouver, BC. Broadly speaking, it’s a bet on Silver, with a wildcard factor that depends on how promising the exploration of its properties turns out.

To give you some background, DVS has big institutional investors as 50% of the shares are owned by firms like Fidelity, Sprott, US, Global Investors, and Delbrook Capital. Also, 15% of the ownership corresponds to Hecla Mining and another 15% to Fury Gold Mines. Lastly, Eric Sprott, a prominent mining investor, owns 9.9%. This leaves roughly 10% left for retail investors, which trades under the ticker DVS on the NYSE-A.

Having said that, DVS justifies its focus on Silver due to several important reasons. The first and most evident is that commodities like Gold and Silver have appreciated significantly since 2024. In fact, the iShares Silver Trust ETF (SLV) is up roughly 59% since January 2024. In fact, YTD the SLV ETF is up already approximately 24%, and has outperformed the S&P 500 SPDR ETF (SPY) by a wide margin in 2025.

It’s worth mentioning that Silver is increasingly becoming more prevalent in solar panels, electronics, and clean energy technologies as well. These are growing demand tailwinds for this mineral. And the consumption from the solar industry alone was 215 million ounces last year, up 11% compared to the previous year. Similarly, the supply of Silver seems to be experiencing around a 215 million ounce shortage, which should only compound its bullish momentum.

The peculiar aspect about the mining of this metal is that most precious metal companies produce Silver mostly as a by-product of mining Gold or other metals. However, DVS, unlike most of its peers, is actually a pure-play Silver company. In fact, DVS’s primary asset is the Kitsault Valley Project in British Columbia’s Golden Triangle. This project covers 163 km², which comprises Dolly Varden, which has a historically noteworthy production of high-grade Silver. DVS also owns Homestake Ridge, with high-grade Silver and Gold. Lastly, DVS’s properties include Torbrit, which was an active Silver mine, and Big Bulk, which could have Copper and Gold veins.

Interestingly, DVS acquired Homestake Ridge from Fury Gold Mines (one of DVS’s larger shareholders today) for $39.2 million. And indeed, Fury kept an ownership stake in DVS as part of the deal. This acquisition was strategic because by buying this nearby deposit, DVS combined Gold and Silver resources into one larger project area in Kitsault Valley. In theory, this could generate synergies for DVS, as exploring and mining a unified property could be more cost-effective.

Valuation Analysis

Now, from a valuation perspective, DVS trades at a $285.8 million market cap, so it remains a relatively smaller player in its sector nonetheless. Note that I’ll be converting the figures in DVS’s latest report from Canadian Dollars [CAD] to USD at the current $0.73 exchange rate. In Q1, their balance sheet held $10.4 million in cash and $13.3 million in short-term investments. In total that amounts to $23.7 million in liquid resources to fund their operations against no financial debt other than regular operating liabilities.

Also, their book value reached $73.2 million largely because most of its assets are in PPE, Deposits, and Exploration and evaluation, which combined amount to $76.3 million. In any case, DVS’s P/B is around 3.9, which does seem notably slightly higher than its peers’ median P/B of just 1.7. Likewise, it’s important to remember that DVS remains a pre-revenue company, as it’s still in the exploration phase of its properties. This means that new potential discoveries within its properties are largely its main value driver today.

With that in mind, note that the Kitsault Valley Trend is technically a 15-kilometer mineralized corridor in British Columbia. This means it contains multiple high-grade Silver and Gold zones. Key deposits include Homestake Main, Homestake Silver, South Reef, Chance, Moose, Wolf, North Star, Torbrit, and Dolly Varden. And its soil geochemistry and magnetic maps highlight strong Gold and Silver anomalies and structural features that could help guide any future drilling.

For example, the Wolf Vein System has been extended southwest and northeast for a total plunge length of 950 meters and remains open at depth. Drilling at Wolf continues to return high Silver grades, such as 1,499 g/t Ag over 15.90 meters and 654 g/t Ag over 21.48 meters. In addition to Wolf, the Moose and Chance veins are also new targets with strong Silver results. Moose returned 977 g/t Ag over 5.00 meters, including 3,670 g/t Ag over 0.79 meters. Chance hit 206 g/t Ag over 23.03 meters, including higher-grade sections.

As for Homestake Silver, this section shows wide intercepts of both Gold and Silver, such as 46.31 g/t Au and 70 g/t Ag over 25 meters and other long intervals with combined grades. DVS is currently testing connections between deposits, such as the Wolf and Torbrit/Kitsol zones, to see if there are other larger continuous mineralized systems.

DVS 2025 plans also include another 35,000 meters of diamond drilling with four rigs. Management disclosed they will focus on expanding high-grade plunges, connecting zones, and testing new targets. Current mineral resources include 34.7 million ounces of indicated Silver and 29.3 million ounces inferred. Additionally, in Homestake Ridge, there are 166,000 ounces of indicated Gold and 817,000 ounces of inferred Gold.

Caveats And Risk Analysis

Having said that, I estimate that in Q1 2025, DVS burned through $1.4 million as they funded these operations. Note that I got this figure by simply adding their latest quarterly cash flows from operations and CAPEX, then converted it to USD as previously mentioned. In any event, since I calculated they hold about $23.7 million in liquid resources, their current burn rate suggests a cash runway of approximately 16.9 quarters (or 4.2 years). This seems more than enough for the foreseeable future and does de-risk it significantly from an investment angle.

Likewise, I think DVS could become an interesting M&A target itself if its reserves prove to be highly valuable. Though no one knows for sure whether or not this will play out, so it remains it’s an inherent risk for investors as well. Therefore, the main risks I see with DVS are that, as you might imagine, there’s no guarantee they’ll be successful in their explorations. It’s unlikely that every drilling will hit a mineralized deposit, and those that do yield something might disappoint investors. In fact, DVS itself notes that “mineral resources are not mineral reserves.”

On top of that, it’s impossible to predict the prices of commodities like Silver. And if the price of Silver declines significantly going forward, DVS stock will most likely follow suit as its prospects are intrinsically tied to that. And historically, DVS has been significantly more volatile than simply buying the SLV ETF.

For the past year, DVS has mostly correlated with the SLV ETF. It seems to me that DVS could be a compelling bet whenever it trails SLV by a wide margin. Conversely, whenever DVS meaningfully outperforms SLV, it might be good for taking profits. However, after the recent rally for DVS stock, it appears that both assets are roughly in line for now, so I don’t see a major opportunity from that perspective. Likewise, DVS’s P/B also shows it’s already at a considerable premium relative to its peers. Thus, DVS stock doesn’t look like the best way to bet on Silver at this valuation, which is why I rate it a “Hold.”

Conclusion: “Hold” For Now

Overall, DVS is an interesting way of betting on the price of Silver. It does have promising properties, and there’s always the wildcard potential of new, exciting discoveries that could re-rate its assets higher (or lower). However, DVS stock has risen considerably since early 2025, and it appears roughly aligned with the appreciation in Silver as well. Their valuation multiples also suggest the stock might be a bit too expensive today. So, on balance, I think DVS could be a viable investment whenever it lags the price of Silver by a considerable margin. Until that happens, I think it’s safer to pass up on DVS.

data.title: PCY: Better Options Exist
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Thesis

The Invesco Emerging Markets Sovereign Debt ETF (NYSEARCA:PCY) is a passively managed fund from Invesco that tracks government bonds in emerging markets with maturity of at least three years. However, there are better-performing funds with similar strategies, with other options realizing a higher total return, a similar total return with lower duration and credit risk, or better potential returns in the event of a shift in bond yields.

PCY's Core Holdings

PCY's holdings are primarily from small, underdeveloped countries, such as Panama, Trinidad and Tobago, and Romania:

Like most bond funds with duration exposure, it has performed poorly recently, with a total return of just over 1% over five years. I'm sympathetic to the contrarian perspective that now that bonds are out of favor, they are poised to bounce back. However, even for investors seeking exposure to fixed income, it's not entirely clear to me what could make PCY appealing, due to a history of underperformance and other superior options for a variety of investor strategies.

Peer Performance

I'm going to compare PCY to similar funds, such as Vanguard Emerging Markets Government Bond Index Fund (VWOB), SPDR® DoubleLine® Emerging Markets Fixed Income ETF (EMTL). I'll also be looking at bond ETFs with somewhat different strategies, such as iShares J.P. Morgan EM High Yield Bond ETF (EMHY), and Vanguard Long-Term Corporate Bond Index Fund ETF (VCLT).

As you can see above, PCY's total return during the past 10 years is the worst of all five bond funds. At no time interval is the performance the best, and at most intervals it is the worst or second worst performing fund. The expense ratio is average, at 0.50%. Meanwhile, the current and 4-year average yield is the second highest, currently at 6.56% TTM.

Evaluating Bond Strategies

Choosing the ideal fund for your particular investing strategy can be difficult with so many factors to measure. However, in my opinion, PCY's composition and performance make it an inferior choice to one or more of the four alternatives mentioned above. This is true across several different metrics and strategies.

High-Yield Strategy:

Over the past 10 years, EMHY has the best total return. This is to be expected, since over the past 25 years, high yield bonds have generally outperformed other bond categories. This is in part due to the fact that high yield bonds have less duration risk, but it is also because the extra premium paid for "risk" has more than compensated investors for higher defaults. I do not believe that this is because we've had a relatively healthy global economy during this time: we had the aftermath of the dot-com meltdown, the 2008 financial collapse, the Covid recession, and the recent dual shocks of inflation and monetary tightening.

Despite all that, high-yield bonds still outperformed. This leads me to the conclusion that bond investors are over-valuing and over-paying for "safety." Even if you expect the future to be as rocky as the past, my expectation is that high yield will continue to outperform, and thus EMHY and other high yield funds will continue to have better total returns than PCY.

"Safe" Strategy:

If you are unconvinced by the argument above and seek a bond fund with a relatively high yield and a high credit rating, then in my opinion, EMTL is a better option. Across 10Y and 5Y, EMTL has a higher total return than PCY, while the two funds are roughly even YTD. However, as you can see below, 53% of EMTL (left) is investment grade, while 45% of PCY (right) is investment grade:

Duration Strategy:

If you are a very contrarian investor, and you're interested in bond exposure because you believe that there will be a shift in long-term yields, you may be more attracted to PCY than EMTL, since PCY has longer duration. In fact, PCY has the longest effective duration of any of the emerging market bond ETFs I evaluated. However, there are other ETFs with both relatively high yields and more duration exposure, such as VCLT. Below, you can see that VCLT (left) has an 82.5% duration of 15+ years, while PCY (right) has a 76.8% duration of 15% years:

It's also possible to invest purely for duration and limit exposure to credit risk by investing in bonds of more credit-worthy countries, such as the US. For example, the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT) has effectively a 100% duration of 15+ years.

The Verdict

Investors may seek out bond exposure for a variety of reasons and strategies, and they can evaluate their options through a variety of metrics: current yield, historical total return, high plus high credit rating, and long duration. However, no matter which metric is used, PCY's performance and portfolio come up short. Since there are better options, I see no reason to hold PCY. I therefore rate PCY a Sell.
